blocks.text: Re: Channel Strategy for Stark-Wilson
Dear Ms. Gonzalez:

This letter sets out the terms on which we will examine the channel strategy of {{fact:f:E-2020-002.client}} and records our agreement to carry the work out. The question you have put to us is a single one: through which channels should Stark-Wilson reach its market, and on what commercial terms, so that the company grows where growth pays and stops spending to hold volume that costs more to serve than it returns. Below you will find the scope, the people who will carry it, the fee and the start, and the standard terms our work runs under, so that both sides begin from one understanding of it. Once you have countersigned and returned a copy, this letter is the agreement between us.
What the work covers

The engagement looks at how Stark-Wilson sells and delivers across its channels, and at where the present mix helps or hinders the results the business wants. We will begin by setting the channels out as they stand: the customers each one reaches, the volume and margin it carries, and the full cost to serve behind it, both direct and indirect. Against that picture we will find where the current arrangement wins business worth having and where it holds on to business at a price the company would not choose if the whole cost were in view. From there we will recommend a channel strategy: which channels to lead with, which to hold, and which to change or step back from, together with what each choice asks of pricing, of sales coverage, and of support. We will show the reasoning under every recommendation, and we will say plainly where the underlying data is thinner than we would like.

Two matters sit outside this letter. We are not retained to carry out the changes we recommend; our work ends with a strategy and a plan your own people can act on, and any help putting it into effect we would agree under a separate letter. We are also not reviewing the individual contracts you hold with particular customers or distributors, which is a distinct question we would open separately if you wished it examined. Where the work runs up against something we are not engaged to answer, we will say so and stop, instead of guessing at it.
The team

Deborah Jimenez, Engagement Manager, will run the engagement day to day and will be your point of contact on anything touching its conduct or its timing. Joseph Reed, Research Associate, will build the channel, margin, and cost-to-serve analysis the recommendations rest on, and will prepare the written record you receive between our reviews. I will stay close to the work and will read what leaves this office under the firm's name, as I do with everything we send a client. You should expect working sessions with your team, minutes of those sessions, status reports written for you, and a briefing to your wider group once the analysis is firm enough to stand behind. We will not change the people on your engagement without telling you first.
Fee and start

Our fee for the engagement is a fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2020-002.fee}}, which covers the whole of the work described above. It is a fixed sum and not a rate billed against hours, and it will not move unless the scope itself changes and we set that change down in writing. We propose to begin on {{fact:f:E-2020-002.start}}, subject to your countersignature and to our receiving the channel sales records and the cost data the analysis needs. We expect the work to run for roughly five months from the start, with the pace of the later stages depending in part on how quickly your team can put those records in front of us.
Fees and payment

We invoice the fixed fee in equal monthly installments across the term, each invoice payable within thirty days of its date. Reasonable expenses incurred at your request, such as travel, are billed at cost and shown separately from the fee; we will not commit any single expense of consequence without your agreement first. Work outside the scope set out above is not begun on assumption. If you ask for something this letter does not cover, we will quote it and wait for your written authorization before we start.
Term and termination

The engagement begins on the start date named above and continues until the work is complete and our final invoice paid. Either of us may end it on fifteen days' written notice to the other. If the engagement ends early, you owe the fee earned for the work performed to the effective date of the notice, together with expenses already committed, and nothing beyond that. We will hand over our work product in the state it then stands, with a written note of what remains unfinished; an unfinished analysis is not a recommendation and should not be read as one.
Confidentiality

We will hold the information you give us in confidence and use it only for this engagement. Access is limited to the team named above and to those in our office whose work requires it. We treat your commercial and operating data as confidential whether or not it is marked so, and we will return or destroy it at your request once the work is done. We will not disclose it outside the firm without your written consent, except where a court or a regulator with jurisdiction compels us, and in that event we will tell you first if we are free to do so. This obligation outlasts the end of the engagement.
Limitation of liability

Our responsibility is to bring professional care and judgment to the analysis and the advice. The decisions that follow from it, and the results of acting on them, rest with {{fact:f:E-2020-002.client}}. Except in the case of our own fraud or willful misconduct, our total liability arising out of this engagement, whether framed in contract or in tort, will not exceed the fees you pay us under it, and neither of us will be liable to the other for indirect or consequential loss. Our analysis rests on the accuracy of what you give us; where that information proves materially wrong, the consequences of the error are not ours to carry, though we will tell you promptly when we have reason to doubt what we have been handed.
Governing law

This agreement is governed by the laws of the state in which our principal office sits, without regard to its conflict-of-laws rules. Before either of us takes a dispute to another forum, we will try in good faith to settle it between senior people on both sides. This letter is the entire agreement between us on its subject and supersedes anything said before it, and it may be amended only in writing signed by both of us.

If these terms reflect your understanding, please sign both copies where indicated, keep one for your file, and return the other to this office. If any part of them does not, telephone me and we will settle it before you sign. We are glad to be taking on this work for Stark-Wilson, and we will be ready to begin as soon as the letter is back.
Very truly yours,

To: Deborah Jimenez
From: Joseph Reed
Re: Channel Strategy for Stark-Wilson, kickoff and plan of work

The letter is countersigned and the engagement begins on {{fact:f:E-2020-002.start}}. This memo sets down what we are doing and how the work divides, so that we start from one plan rather than assemble it as we go. Our task is to work out how {{fact:f:E-2020-002.client}} should sell and deliver across its channels: which channels carry which customers, what each one earns after the full cost of serving it, and which the company should lead with, hold, or change. Everything we recommend has to be built from Stark-Wilson's own sales and cost records, so that when we put a channel decision in front of them the evidence for it is theirs and not ours. The engagement runs about five months, so the early weeks carry weight: the baseline has to be right before the analysis can move. Deborah Jimenez runs the engagement and owns the line to the client; our client contact is Julie Gonzalez, Director of Operations, and requests to the client go through Deborah, not around her.
How the work divides

items: 1. Channel map and baseline. I will set out every channel the company sells through and pull the last two to three years of sales by channel, product line, and customer segment, so we have a single picture of what moves where. This is the foundation the rest depends on, and I will not build anything on top of it until it reconciles to the finance records.

2. Cost to serve. For each channel I will assemble the full cost of serving it, direct and indirect: sales coverage, order handling, logistics, returns, and support. Margin by channel means little until the cost to serve is set against it, so the two are built together.
3. Customer and segment view. I will map which customers each channel reaches and where the same customer is served through more than one, so we can see the overlap and the cost it carries.

4. Channel economics and options. Once the baseline and the cost to serve hold, we frame the options: which channels the numbers say to lead with, which to hold, and which to change or step back from. Deborah Jimenez and I take this together, and nothing here goes to the client before the baseline supports it.
5. Recommendation and roadmap. We turn the chosen direction into a plan Stark-Wilson can act on, with the sequence of changes and what each one asks of pricing, coverage, and support.

blocks.text: First weeks

items: I will draft the data request this week: sales by channel and product, the cost lines behind each channel, and the field definitions attached, so the finance team and we are counting the same things. Deborah Jimenez reviews it before it goes to Julie Gonzalez.
We agree the list of people we need to interview inside the client before we ask for a single calendar slot, so access is settled once and not renegotiated later.
I will set up the first working session with Julie Gonzalez and her team. Given how sessions are running now, we should plan for that one to be held by video.
Nothing about any channel is characterized to anyone at Stark-Wilson until the baseline reconciles.

blocks.text: The one thing I need settled this week is the data request and the interview list, both agreed with Deborah Jimenez and both sent to Julie Gonzalez. If the sales records come in clean, the baseline is the fast part; if they do not, that is where the schedule will be decided, and finding that out early is the point. I will circulate the data request in draft on Wednesday.
Julie Gonzalez, with Deborah Jimenez copied:

Thank you for the first extract. We have the sales-by-channel file and the product breakout, and I have started reconciling the channel totals against the finance summary you sent last week. So far they tie at the channel level; it is the split within the distributor channel that I am still squaring, and I may have a question or two for your finance team once I have narrowed it down. I would rather get the reconciliation solid before we build anything on it.

The one thing still outstanding is the cost-to-serve data: the order-handling, logistics, and support costs behind each channel. We can begin the baseline without it, but the picture of {{fact:f:E-2020-002.client}} stays half-drawn until the cost side is in, because margin by channel says little on its own. If your team can send whatever exists in its current form, even rough, we will work with it and tell you where the gaps are.

I would also like to hold the first working session in the next two weeks, by video, to walk you and your team through the Stark-Wilson channel baseline as it stands. It will be a review of the numbers, not a set of conclusions; those come later, once the cost side is complete. Deborah Jimenez will send two or three options for the time. Nothing else is needed from you today.
Joseph Reed
Working Session: Channel Baseline and Cost-to-Serve Review

Minutes recorded by Deborah Jimenez, Engagement Manager, Calderwood Partners. Working session on the Channel Strategy for Stark-Wilson, held by video with the client. Corrections should reach this office within five business days. Absent corrections, these minutes stand as the record of the session.
Attendees

items: Deborah Jimenez, Engagement Manager, Calderwood Partners
Joseph Reed, Research Associate, Calderwood Partners
Julie Gonzalez, Director of Operations, Stark-Wilson

blocks.text: Channel baseline

Joseph Reed presented the channel baseline. It covers three years of sales for {{fact:f:E-2020-002.client}} across the four channels: direct, distributor, reseller, and online. Sales by channel are reconciled to the finance records at the channel level. The split within the distributor channel is not yet final. One region books distributor and direct sales to the same account, and those entries are being separated. The direct, reseller, and online channels tie cleanly. Julie Gonzalez confirmed that the account in question is served both ways by design and gave the names of the two managers who handle it. Joseph Reed logged the correction.
Cost to serve

The cost-to-serve data is partial. Order-handling and logistics costs are in for all four channels. Support costs are in for the direct channel only. Joseph Reed noted that margin by channel cannot be stated until the support costs are complete, because the support cost is the line that separates the channels most. The early reading is that the reseller channel carries a higher cost to serve than its share of margin, driven by returns and pre-sale support, but this is provisional and rests on the costs received so far. No conclusion was drawn. Julie Gonzalez asked how the online channel compared; Joseph Reed said the order-handling cost there is the lowest of the four, but the figure is incomplete without the support side. Julie Gonzalez agreed to have the remaining support costs sent by the end of next week and to route the request through her finance team so the definitions match the earlier extract.
Scope and timing

Deborah Jimenez confirmed the scope is unchanged: the analysis covers the four channels and stops at a strategy and a roadmap, not at implementation. Julie Gonzalez asked whether the customer contracts would be reviewed. They are outside this engagement and were not opened. The next working session was set for two weeks out, to be held by video, and will cover the completed cost-to-serve view and the channel economics. Julie Gonzalez asked that the channels not be characterized to her sales managers before that session, so the review stays diagnostic. The team agreed.
Risks noted

Two risks were raised, each with the step being taken on it. First, the support-cost gap holds up the channel economics; the analysis waits on the client data, and Julie Gonzalez has committed a date for it. Second, the distributor-direct overlap may be wider than the single account found so far; Joseph Reed will test the other large accounts for the same pattern. Both were recorded with an owner and a date so neither slips.
Action items

rows: Send the remaining support costs, by channel
Separate distributor and direct sales for the shared account and remap the region
Test the other major accounts for distributor-direct overlap
Hold the provisional cost-to-serve reading inside the team until the support costs are complete

blocks.text: The next working session will review the completed cost-to-serve picture and the channel economics that follow from it. Deborah Jimenez will circulate the revised baseline once the shared-account correction is made.
Status Report: Channel Strategy for Stark-Wilson

rows: Joseph Reed, Research Associate, Calderwood Partners

blocks.text: Summary

This report covers the first half of the channel strategy work for Stark-Wilson and sets out where the engagement stands. The channel baseline is finished and reconciled to your finance records. The cost-to-serve analysis is built for three of the four channels, with one set of support costs still to come. Once those arrive we can state margin against the full cost of serving each channel, which is what the analysis has been assembling toward. The pace is in line with the plan for the engagement to this point.
Work completed to date

The first months produced the evidence the recommendations will rest on. Every figure is reconciled to your own sales and finance records rather than to anything we carried in, so each number traces back to a source your team already knows. The following are complete:

items: A channel baseline covering three years of sales across every channel, reconciled to the finance summary at the channel level.
A cost-to-serve model for the direct, distributor, and reseller channels, built from order-handling, logistics, and support costs rather than from a flat allocation.
A customer view showing where the same account is served through more than one channel, and a first measure of what that overlap costs.
A corrected split of the distributor and direct sales that had been booked together for one region, so the channel totals now hold.

blocks.text: An early pattern

One early finding is worth stating plainly, with the caution that it is not yet final. On the costs received so far, the reseller channel carries a higher cost to serve than its share of margin supports, driven largely by returns and pre-sale support, while the direct channel earns more after cost than its volume alone would suggest. The online channel looks the cheapest to serve on the order side, but its support cost is the piece still outstanding, so its position is not yet settled. This is the pattern the second half will test and either confirm or qualify. It is not a recommendation, and no channel decision should be read into it yet.
Work still ahead
With the baseline fixed, the remaining work turns the analysis into a strategy the business can act on. Assuming the outstanding support costs arrive as scheduled, what is left is:

items: Complete the cost-to-serve picture for the last channel and close the margin-after-cost view across all four.
Frame the channel options: which channels the numbers support leading with, which to hold, and which to change or step back from.
Set out the roadmap, with the sequence of changes and what each move asks of pricing, sales coverage, and support.
Bring the findings to the next working session for discussion before anything is settled.

blocks.text: Fee and budget

The engagement is tracking within the fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2020-002.fee}} agreed at the outset, and roughly half of the planned effort has been used, in line with where the work stands. Nothing in the plan for the second half calls for more than was scoped. If the analysis surfaces a question that genuinely falls outside the original scope, Deborah Jimenez will bring it to you before any work is done against it, so the fee holds no surprise at the end.
Risks being managed
Three risks are worth watching. Each is named here with the step being taken on it.

items: The outstanding support costs. The channel economics cannot be closed until they arrive; the analysis waits on the data, not on us. Your team has committed to send them by the end of next week.

The extent of the channel overlap. One shared account has been corrected, and there may be others; we are testing the larger accounts for the same pattern. If the overlap is wider, the cost attached to it grows with it.

Decision latency. The second half has a critical path, and the channel choices we bring to your leadership set the pace of the roadmap that follows. Delay in those choices pushes the roadmap out by the same amount.

blocks.text: What we need from Stark-Wilson

Two things keep the second half on schedule: the remaining support costs by the end of next week, and a working session in the following week to walk your team through the channel economics once they are complete. Deborah Jimenez leads the engagement and will set the session time with your office. Questions on the analysis can come to me directly.
